briefing.headline: Rig up a Medium Diving Crankbait — steady summer approach for stained water.
briefing.gameplan:
The Medium Diving Crankbait is a strong choice for summer with stained water and stable conditions.

Wheeler: The DT-6 is the most universal crankbait; it covers the 3-6 foot sweet spot and works from mid-40s water temps through pre-spawn, post-spawn, summer, and fall on rivers, highland and lowland impoundments, and smallmouth lakes.

Key adjustment for stable: moderate cadence — match the natural pace of local forage.

primaryApproach.retrieve: Wheeler: The DT-6 is the most universal crankbait; it covers the 3-6 foot sweet spot and works from mid-40s water temps through pre-spawn, post-spawn, summer, and fall on rivers, highland and lowland
primaryApproach.targets: Focus on grass, ledge, flat. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

alternateApproach.retrieve: KVD: Position boat outside the grass line, fire at a 45° quartering angle into shallower grass. Crank down into the grass, slow up — let the bait float free or rip it free. That stop-start-rip action
alternateApproach.targets: Focus on grass, ledge, flat. Work the transitions and edges where structure changes.

proInsights.insight: KVD: At Toledo Bend, started each morning shallow on the shad spawn for 1-1.5 hours, then transitioned offshore to deep crank. The biggest fish all came deep cranking with 6XD, 8XD, 10XD. Didn't get a ton of bites but when he got bit, it was a giant.
Wheeler: Best offshore spots included areas adjacent to grass lines, actual holes in the grass, and hard-bottomed shell beds in 8-35 feet of water.
Johnston: On the St. Lawrence, fish a mix of rock, sand, and weed in 15 feet or less. Hit as many spots as you can and hope the right ones bite.

briefing.depthStrategy: Stable summer patterns are predictable. Dawn/dusk fish are shallow (3-8ft) near cover. Midday fish pull to offshore structure at 12-25ft — ledges, humps, brush piles, and creek channel bends.
adjustIf: If conditions suddenly shift (pressure drop, wind change), be ready to speed up your approach — transitional moments produce big bites
If water clears significantly, downsize presentations and move to natural color patterns
If fish are short-striking, add a long pause after deflections to let the lure suspend
